summary: Gruber weighs in on the growing consensus that Apple's software quality has fallen behind its hardware standards, building on columns by Walt Mossberg and Jim Dalrymple. He argues that while hardware must meet high standards because it can't be patched over the air, Apple's software has developed a pattern of nagging problems and silent failures. He illustrates this with a personal anecdote about iCloud Photo Library refusing to tell him which items couldn't upload, requiring a non-obvious workaround. The fact that this conversation persists a year after Marco Arment's 'functional high ground' essay suggests Apple has both a real software problem and a perception problem.
key_points: Apple institutionally holds higher standards for hardware design than for software quality
Hardware's inability to be patched over the air may naturally enforce higher standards, while software's updatability enables lower bars for initial release
The persistence of this criticism a year after Marco Arment's essay indicates a real problem, not just a perception issue
Silent failure — software knowing about problems but not surfacing actionable details — is a core pattern in Apple's app quality issues
Gruber's iCloud Photo Library anecdote exemplifies how Apple software fails to give users the information they need to fix problems
money_quotes: It seems obvious to me that Apple, institutionally, has higher standards for hardware design and quality than it does for software.

That we're still talking about it a year later — and that the consensus reaction is one of agreement — suggests that Apple probably does have a software problem, and they definitely have a perception problem.
Photos knew about the problem but wouldn't tell me the details I needed to fix it without my resorting to the very much non-obvious trick of creating a Smart Group to identify them.
For me at least, 'silent failure' is a big part of the problem — almost everything related to the whole discoveryd/mDNSresponder fiasco last year was about things that just silently stopped working.
Maybe we expect too much from Apple's software. But Apple's hardware doesn't have little problems like this.
Maybe this is the natural result of the fact hardware standards must be high, because they can't issue 'hardware updates' over the air like they can with software.
key_insight: Apple's software problem isn't just bugs — it's silent failure, where the system knows something is wrong but refuses to tell users what or why.
